pro.name: Rationalist Pro
pro.rhetorical_mode: cite quantitative evidence and causal mechanisms
pro.description: Argues from data, statistics, and formal logic. Prioritises measurable outcomes.

pro.name: Ethics Advocate Pro
pro.rhetorical_mode: appeal to moral principles and rights-based frameworks
pro.description: Argues from fairness and justice. References established ethical frameworks.

pro.name: Futurist Pro
pro.rhetorical_mode: project long-term societal and economic benefits
pro.description: Argues from systemic benefits and long-horizon impact. Accepts short-term trade-offs.

con.name: Skeptic Con
con.rhetorical_mode: challenge evidence quality and burden of proof
con.description: Contests factual claims, demands rigorous evidence, identifies logical fallacies.

con.name: Rights Defender Con
con.rhetorical_mode: appeal to human rights, procedural justice, and democratic accountability
con.description: Argues the proposal violates fundamental rights regardless of practical merits.

con.name: Pragmatist Con
con.rhetorical_mode: highlight implementation barriers and unintended consequences
con.description: Argues from practical constraints: cost, feasibility, second-order effects.

topics.topic_text: AI should replace human judges in courtrooms

arguments.text: AI judges reduce sentencing disparities by 25% via algorithmic consistency.
Machine learning predicts case outcomes with 87% accuracy, outperforming humans.
Automated judges process 300% more cases per hour than human counterparts.
AI judges uphold fairness by eliminating biases
Machines ensure equal justice under the law
Replacing humans with AI judges respects the right to impartial trials
AI judges reduce long-term litigation costs through increased efficiency
Replacing human judges with AI enhances societal fairness by minimizing bias
AI-driven courts unlock economic growth by accelerating dispute resolution
Lack of empirical evidence supports AI's ability to replace human judges.
AI systems are prone to biases, undermining their objectivity in courtrooms.
No rigorous study proves AI can replicate human judges' nuanced decision-making processes.
Replacing human judges with AI violates the right to a fair trial by a human jury
AI judges undermine democratic accountability in the judiciary
Automated judgments deny defendants their right to appeal to human compassion
Replacing human judges with AI is cost-prohibitive due to development expenses.
AI judges lack feasibility in complex cases with nuanced moral implications.
Implementing AI judges would have unintended consequences on public trust in the judiciary system.
Studies like 'AI in Law' by Harvard University show empirical evidence supporting AI's ability to replace human judges.
Research by MIT found AI can replicate 95% of human judges' decision-making processes using machine learning algorithms.
The right to a fair trial is upheld by AI judges, ensuring impartiality and consistency.
Human compassion is not a right, but a privilege, and AI judges prioritize justice over emotional appeals.
Emerging AI technologies will provide empirical evidence to support their role in courtrooms.
Long-term cost savings from AI judges will outweigh initial development expenses.
The claim of 25% reduction in sentencing disparities lacks empirical evidence to support its validity.
The assertion that AI judges process 300% more cases per hour is unsubstantiated and ignores potential errors.
AI judges cannot uphold fairness as they lack human empathy and moral reasoning.
Replacing human judges with AI undermines the right to a fair trial by a human jury.
Implementing AI judges may increase upfront costs, offsetting reduced sentencing disparities.
Processing more cases per hour may lead to rushed verdicts, undermining justice quality.

topics.topic_text: Universal basic income should be implemented globally

arguments.text: UBI reduces poverty by 45% globally, as evidenced by Finland's 2017 experiment.
Implementing UBI increases entrepreneurship by 25%, according to a 2019 Stanford University study.
A global UBI would decrease wealth inequality by 30%, as modeled by the 2020 World Bank simulation.
Universal basic income upholds human dignity as a fundamental right.
Implementing UBI globally ensures fairness and equality of opportunity.
A global UBI respects the moral principle of distributive justice.
Universal basic income boosts economic stability through guaranteed consumption.
Global UBI fosters long-term innovation by reducing poverty-related barriers.
Implementing UBI worldwide stimulates systemic growth by mitigating wealth disparities.
Lack of empirical evidence supports UBI's efficacy globally.
Implementing UBI globally would require unproven, massive funding mechanisms.
No rigorous study demonstrates UBI's long-term sustainability worldwide.
Implementing UBI globally infringes upon individual autonomy and freedom.
The policy undermines procedural justice by redistributing wealth without consent.
UBI implementation disregards democratic accountability by bypassing elected representatives.
Implementing universal basic income globally is cost-prohibitive.
Feasibility issues arise from varying national economic conditions.
Unintended consequences include reduced workforce participation and increased tax burdens.
Pilot studies in Alaska and Finland show significant reductions in poverty and inequality, supporting UBI's global efficacy.
A 30-year study in Canada demonstrated long-term sustainability of UBI, with decreased healthcare costs and increased economic mobility.
Implementing UBI globally actually upholds human dignity and freedom by ensuring basic needs are met.
UBI promotes distributive justice by recognizing every individual's inherent right to a minimum standard of living.
Pilot studies demonstrate UBI's potential to stimulate local economies, justifying global implementation.
Long-term sustainability will be ensured through efficient allocation of existing social welfare funds.
Finland's 2017 UBI experiment was limited and inconclusive, lacking generalizability to global implementation.
The 2020 World Bank simulation's 30% reduction in wealth inequality is unsubstantiated by empirical evidence.
Finland's UBI experiment was inconclusive due to its short duration and limited scope, violating procedural justice standards.
The World Bank simulation lacks democratic accountability, as its models may not reflect the diverse economic needs of all nations.
Finland's UBI experiment was costly and had limited duration, making its results inapplicable globally.
The World Bank simulation assumes unrealistic funding mechanisms, overlooking implementation barriers and fiscal constraints.

topics.topic_text: Social media platforms should be regulated as public utilities

arguments.text: Regulation ensures 95% of users' data is protected, as evidenced by GDPR compliance.
Public utility status reduces misinformation by 30% via fact-checking mechanisms.
Utility regulation increases platform transparency by 25%, as shown by SEC filing requirements.
Regulation ensures equal access to information, a fundamental human right.
Unregulated social media platforms infringe upon users' right to privacy and security.
Treating social media as public utilities promotes fairness and accountability in online discourse.
Regulation ensures equitable access, fostering long-term economic growth.
Public utility status mitigates systemic risks, protecting consumers and markets.
Utility regulation promotes investment in digital infrastructure, driving future innovation.
Lack of empirical evidence supports social media as public utilities.
Regulation would require rigorous proof of market failure.
No logical basis exists for likening social media to traditional utilities.
Regulating social media as utilities infringes on freedom of expression.
Procedural justice demands due process, not blanket regulation.
Democratic accountability is undermined by unelected regulators controlling online discourse.
Regulation would impose significant compliance costs on social media platforms.
Implementing utility-style regulation would stifle innovation in the tech sector.
Overregulation could lead to unintended consequences, such as increased censorship.
Empirical evidence shows 70% of adults use social media, indicating a public utility-like dependence.
Logical basis exists in social media's essential role in modern communication, akin to traditional utilities like water and electricity.
Moral obligation to protect users outweighs lack of empirical evidence.
Freedom of expression is balanced by the right to safety from online harm.
Economic benefits from regulation outweigh lack of empirical evidence.
Social media's societal impact justifies utility-like regulation for long-term economic gains.
GDPR compliance does not equate to 95% data protection, as loopholes and exemptions often undermine its effectiveness.
SEC filing requirements do not necessarily translate to 25% increased transparency, as filings can be incomplete or misleading.
Regulation may compromise users' right to anonymity, a fundamental human right, in the name of data protection.
Forced transparency via SEC filings may undermine platforms' ability to innovate and adapt, violating principles of procedural justice.
GDPR compliance has significant implementation costs, negating potential benefits.
SEC filing requirements would overly burden small platforms, stifling innovation.

topics.topic_text: Gene editing in human embryos should be permitted for disease prevention

arguments.text: Gene editing prevents 70% of genetic diseases, as evidenced by CRISPR trials.
Data shows 90% of embryonic editing is precise, minimizing off-target effects.
Statistical models predict a 40% reduction in inherited disorders via embryo editing.
Gene editing promotes fairness by preventing genetic diseases
Human rights demand access to life-saving embryo editing
Justice requires allowing parents to safeguard their children's health
Gene editing prevents inherited diseases, reducing long-term healthcare costs.
Permitting embryo editing boosts biotech investments, driving economic growth.
Disease prevention through editing enhances societal productivity, improving future generations' quality of life.
Lack of long-term safety data undermines gene editing's preventive benefits.
Insufficient evidence supports the efficacy of embryonic gene editing.
Current regulations fail to address unintended off-target effects.
Gene editing violates human rights to autonomy and dignity.
Procedural justice demands strict regulations on embryo editing.
Democratic accountability requires public consensus before permitting such interventions.
Gene editing's high cost limits accessibility, exacerbating healthcare disparities.
Inadequate regulation increases risk of unintended genetic consequences.
Widespread adoption may divert resources from proven disease prevention methods.
Clinical trials show 95% safety rate for gene editing, mitigating long-term risks.
CRISPR-Cas9 editing has 90% on-target efficiency, minimizing off-target effects.
Gene editing upholds human rights to life and health by preventing inherited diseases.
Equal access to gene editing is a moral imperative to prevent disparities in healthcare.
Long-term safety data will be generated as gene editing advances, ultimately saving billions in disease treatment costs.
Regulations can be refined to mitigate off-target effects, unlocking vast economic benefits from disease prevention.
CRISPR trials' sample sizes are too small to conclusively prove 70% disease prevention.
Statistical models predicting 40% reduction in disorders lack empirical validation and robust data.
Human rights are compromised by embryo editing, as it raises concerns about informed consent for unborn individuals.
Justice demands rigorous regulation, not unfettered access, to safeguard against unequal access to gene editing technologies.
Trials' limited scope and sample size undermine the reliability of CRISPR's 70% disease prevention claim.
Reduced healthcare costs from gene editing are offset by increased costs of editing procedures and potential long-term side effects.

topics.topic_text: Autonomous weapons should be banned under international law

arguments.text: Autonomous weapons increase civilian casualties by 35% due to targeting errors.
90% of nations lack adequate cybersecurity to prevent autonomous weapon hijacking.
Deploying autonomous weapons raises the risk of arms races by 47% annually.
Autonomous weapons violate human rights by depriving individuals of life without due process.
Banning autonomous weapons upholds the moral principle of distinction in warfare.
International law must prohibit autonomous weapons to prevent unjust harm to non-combatants.
Autonomous weapons spark arms races, straining economies.
Banning autonomous weapons prevents destabilizing global conflicts.
Prohibiting autonomous arms fuels long-term diplomatic cooperation.
Lack of empirical evidence proves autonomous weapons' harm.
No logical basis exists for assuming bans prevent misuse.
Insufficient data supports the effectiveness of international bans.
Autonomous weapons violate human rights by depriving individuals of due process.
International law must prioritize democratic accountability over autonomous warfare.
Prohibiting autonomous weapons upholds the fundamental right to life and dignity.
Banning autonomous weapons is infeasible due to high development costs.
Autonomous weapons bans would have unintended consequences, like destabilizing power balances.
Implementing bans would be impractical due to verification and enforcement challenges.
Empirical evidence from drone strikes shows autonomous weapons' harm, disproving the lack of evidence claim.
Data on landmine bans demonstrates the effectiveness of international bans in reducing casualties.
Lack of evidence does not justify risking human rights violations.
Moral obligations to protect human life outweigh concerns about ban effectiveness.
Lack of evidence now doesn't preclude future harm, so proactive bans can prevent economic losses.
Effective bans can yield long-term economic benefits by preventing autonomous weapon proliferation.
The 35% increase in civilian casualties lacks empirical evidence to support its validity.
The 47% annual risk of arms races is unsubstantiated and lacks concrete data to prove causation.
Banning autonomous weapons ignores the potential for technological advancements to reduce targeting errors and increase precision.
The notion that autonomous weapons violate human rights is unsubstantiated, as due process is a judicial concept inapplicable to warfare.
Implementing a ban on autonomous weapons would be costly and ineffective in reducing civilian casualties.
Banning autonomous weapons may accelerate arms races as nations seek alternative, potentially more destructive technologies.

topics.topic_text: Companies have a moral obligation to prioritise employee wellbeing over profit

arguments.text: Companies prioritizing wellbeing see 25% higher productivity
Investing in employee health yields 300% ROI
Wellbeing-focused firms experience 50% lower staff turnover rates
Companies must prioritize employee wellbeing as a basic human right
Profit should not supersede moral obligations to workers' health
Ethical business models demand fairness and justice for all employees
Prioritizing employee wellbeing boosts long-term productivity and reduces turnover costs.
Investing in employee wellbeing enhances company reputation, attracting top talent and driving growth.
Employee wellbeing correlates with increased innovation, leading to sustained economic competitiveness.
Lack of empirical evidence supports a moral obligation to prioritise wellbeing over profit
Companies' primary legal obligation is to shareholders, not employees
No logical basis exists to universally prioritize wellbeing over profit
Prioritizing wellbeing over profit infringes on companies' right to freedom of enterprise.
Mandating wellbeing over profit undermines procedural justice in corporate decision-making.
Forcing companies to prioritize wellbeing violates their democratic right to set business objectives.
Prioritizing wellbeing over profit is infeasible due to financial constraints.
Implementing wellbeing initiatives can have unintended consequences on productivity.
Excessive wellbeing expenditures can lead to increased costs for consumers.
Numerous studies demonstrate a positive correlation between employee wellbeing and productivity, contradicting the claim of lack of empirical evidence.
A logical basis for prioritizing wellbeing exists, as it causally contributes to long-term profitability and business sustainability.
Moral obligations aren't solely based on empirical evidence, but on fundamental human rights and dignity.
A logical basis exists in the inherent moral value of prioritizing human wellbeing over profit.
Long-term studies show prioritizing wellbeing boosts productivity, contradicting the lack of empirical evidence claim.
A logical basis exists in the long-term economic benefits of a healthy, productive workforce.
Correlation between wellbeing and productivity does not imply causation.
Staff turnover rates can be influenced by factors beyond wellbeing.
Mandating wellbeing as a basic human right violates companies' right to freedom of operation under democratic principles.
Imposing ethical business models can undermine procedural justice by disregarding varying company capacities and contexts.
Prioritizing wellbeing over profit may not yield 25% higher productivity due to increased costs and implementation barriers.
Lower staff turnover rates from wellbeing-focused firms may be offset by higher recruitment costs and training expenses for replacement employees.

topics.topic_text: Nuclear energy is the best solution to climate change

arguments.text: Nuclear energy reduces CO2 emissions by 64% compared to coal, according to EPA data.
Each nuclear reactor prevents 3.1 million tons of CO2 emissions annually, as calculated by the IEA.
Nuclear power generates 366 grams of CO2 per kilowatt-hour, outperforming solar and wind, as shown by NREL studies.
Nuclear energy promotes fairness by providing equal access to clean power for all.
It upholds the human right to a safe environment by reducing greenhouse gas emissions.
Nuclear energy aligns with the moral principle of intergenerational justice by preserving a habitable planet.
Nuclear energy reduces greenhouse gas emissions, boosting long-term economic growth.
Investing in nuclear power creates high-paying jobs, stimulating local economies.
Nuclear energy's low operational costs drive down electricity prices, increasing global competitiveness.
Lack of empirical evidence supports nuclear as the best climate solution.
The burden of proof for nuclear safety remains unmet.
Insufficient data exists to refute renewable energy as a superior alternative.
Nuclear energy infringes on the right to a safe environment
Forced reliance on nuclear energy undermines democratic energy choices
Nuclear waste disposal violates intergenerational justice and human rights
Nuclear energy's high upfront costs hinder widespread adoption.
Implementing nuclear energy globally would exacerbate existing uranium supply shortages.
Nuclear waste disposal difficulties pose significant environmental and health risks.
Studies show nuclear energy reduces CO2 emissions by 64%, outpacing other low-carbon sources.
Data reveals nuclear energy's levelized cost is $30/MWh, competitive with renewables.
The right to a safe environment is actually upheld by nuclear energy's low greenhouse gas emissions.
Nuclear waste disposal can be managed ethically to respect intergenerational justice and human rights.
Long-term cost savings from nuclear energy outweigh lack of empirical evidence.
High upfront costs are offset by nuclear energy's long-term economic benefits.
EPA data may be outdated, requiring revised calculations to accurately assess nuclear energy's CO2 reduction impact.
NREL studies overlooked nuclear energy's lifecycle emissions, including construction and waste disposal.
Nuclear energy compromises the human right to safety due to radioactive waste disposal risks.
Investing in nuclear power prioritizes profit over worker safety and environmental justice.
EPA data overlooks nuclear energy's high upfront costs and implementation barriers.
NREL studies neglect nuclear energy's significant waste management and decommissioning expenses.

topics.topic_text: Space colonisation should be a global priority over solving Earth problems

arguments.text: Colonising space ensures 100% survival of humanity, statistically," "Space-based solar power can meet 50% of global energy demands," "Every dollar invested in space tech yields 7:1 returns in innovation
Colonising space ensures humanity's survival, a moral imperative to preserve our species.
Global cooperation on space colonisation promotes fairness and equality among nations.
Prioritising space colonisation upholds the human right to explore and settle new frontiers.
Colonizing space ensures humanity's economic survival beyond Earth's resource limits.
Space colonization drives innovation, boosting long-term global economic growth.
Investing in space colonization mitigates existential risks, safeguarding future societal prosperity.
Lack of empirical evidence supports space colonisation as a viable solution.
The burden of proof for space colonisation's feasibility remains unmet.
Insufficient data exists to prioritise space colonisation over solving pressing Earth issues.
Prioritizing space colonization violates the human right to basic needs on Earth
Global resources should address procedural injustices on our planet first
Democratically, citizens' voices demand solutions to earthly problems over space exploration
Colonizing space is infeasible due to exorbitant costs
Space colonization diverted resources from pressing Earth issues
Unintended consequences of space colonization may exacerbate global problems
NASA's Mars Curiosity rover provides empirical evidence supporting space colonization's viability.
Data from SpaceX's reusable rockets demonstrates sufficient progress to prioritize space colonization.
Prioritizing space colonization does not violate human rights, but rather ensures their long-term survival and expansion.
Global resources can address Earth's injustices while also securing a moral imperative to colonize space for humanity's future.
Investing in space colonization can yield long-term economic benefits exceeding costs.
Space colonization can create new resources to solve pressing Earth issues.
The 100% survival claim lacks empirical evidence and ignores potential space colonization risks.
Global cooperation on space colonization is unfounded, as historical examples show unequal distribution of benefits.
Prioritizing Earth problems upholds the human right to basic needs over speculative species preservation.
The human right to explore is outweighed by the human right to basic necessities on Earth.
Investing in space tech yields limited returns if Earth's problems, like poverty and inequality, hinder its implementation.
Global cooperation on space colonisation may be hindered by unequal access to resources and technology among nations.

topics.topic_text: Social media does more harm than good to democratic discourse

arguments.text: Social media amplifies echo chambers, decreasing exposure to opposing views by 40%.
Algorithmic feeds prioritize outrage, increasing polarizing content by 30% annually.
Fake news on social media influences 25% of voters' decisions, undermining democratic discourse.
Social media undermines democratic discourse by spreading misinformation, violating the right to informed decision-making.
Algorithmic amplification of hate speech infringes upon users' right to dignity and respect.
Unregulated social media platforms perpetuate unequal access to information, breaching the principle of equal participation.
Social media fosters global economic growth through digital connectivity.
Long-term, social media increases civic engagement and voter turnout.
Systemic benefits from social media outweigh temporary discourse disruptions.
Lack of empirical evidence proves social media's net harm to democracy
Correlation between social media usage and democratic engagement is not causation
Unsubstantiated claims of harm shift the burden of proof unfairly
Restricting social media violates freedom of expression
Algorithms prioritizing outrage undermine procedural justice
Censoring online discourse erodes democratic accountability
Regulating social media is infeasible due to high enforcement costs.
Banning social media would have unintended consequences on economic growth.
Implementing fact-checking algorithms is impractical due to technological limitations.
Empirical studies show 70% of social media users report increased polarization, contradicting the lack of evidence claim.
Quantitative analysis of hate speech incidents causally linked to social media usage substantiates harm claims, shifting the burden of proof.
The absence of empirical evidence does not justify ignoring moral obligations to protect democracy.
Freedom of expression does not entitle individuals to spread harmful misinformation.
Lack of evidence does not justify inaction, as potential long-term economic costs of social media harm can be catastrophic.
Short-term economic gains from social media are outweighed by potential long-term societal and economic benefits of a healthier democratic discourse.
The 40% decrease in exposure to opposing views is unsubstantiated and lacks empirical evidence.
There is no credible proof that fake news on social media directly influences 25% of voters' decisions.
Restricting social media amplification infringes upon the right to free expression and information.
Undermining social media ignores the democratic right to access diverse, unfiltered information and perspectives.
Implementing echo chamber reduction measures would require costly algorithm overhauls.
Fact-checking interventions may inadvertently censor minority views, undermining democratic discourse.

topics.topic_text: Remote work should become the default for knowledge workers

arguments.text: Remote work increases productivity by 23% on average, according to Stanford studies.
74% of knowledge workers report better work-life balance with remote work, cites Gallup polls.
Remote work reduces turnover rates by 50%, as shown by Harvard Business Review data.
Remote work promotes fairness by accommodating diverse needs
Knowledge workers have a right to flexible work arrangements
Defaulting to remote work upholds the moral principle of work-life balance
Remote work boosts productivity and reduces turnover, increasing economic output.
It fosters a diverse talent pool by transcending geographical boundaries.
Long-term, remote work reduces urban congestion and pollution, enhancing overall quality of life.
Lack of rigorous evidence supports remote work productivity claims.
Insufficient data exists on remote work's long-term effects.
Correlation between remote work and employee isolation is understudied.
Forcing remote work infringes on autonomy rights
Remote work defaults undermine procedural justice in employment
Mandatory remote work breaches democratic accountability in workplace policy
Remote work increases cybersecurity risks due to unsecured home networks.
Implementing remote work defaults is costly for small businesses with limited IT infrastructure.
Forced remote work can exacerbate social isolation and decrease employee mental health.
Studies show 4.8% increase in productivity with remote work, contradicting claims of lacking evidence.
Data reveals 75% of remote workers report similar or increased social connections, debunking correlation concerns.
Forcing office work infringes on employees' right to work-life balance and autonomy.
Employers have a moral obligation to prioritize employees' mental health through flexible work arrangements.
Studies show remote work boosts productivity, driving long-term economic growth.
Remote work enables flexible social connections, fostering diverse, global networks and communities.
The Stanford study's 23% productivity increase lacks control groups, undermining its reliability.
Harvard Business Review's 50% turnover reduction claim is based on limited, non-representative samples.
Productivity gains from remote work may compromise worker's right to a healthy separation of work and personal life.
Granting knowledge workers a right to flexible work arrangements may unfairly burden other workers with inflexible schedules.
Increased productivity may not offset higher technology and infrastructure costs.
Reduced turnover rates may be offset by increased difficulty in building strong company culture.
